title: 1 3 Taylor Series Error Estimates
body:
1.3 TAYLOR SERIES AND ERROR ESTIMATION

The Taylor Series for functions of two variables: The Taylor Series of f (x, y) about the point (a, b) is

∂f ∂f f (x, y) = f (a, b) + (x − a) (a, b) + (y − b) (a, b) ∂x ∂y ∂ 2f 2   1 2∂ f 2 ∂ f + (x − a) (a, b) + 2(x − a)(y − b) (a, b) + (y − b) (a, b) 2! ∂x2 ∂x∂y ∂y 2 + higher-order terms.

You have already seen how Taylor series are used to approximate complicated functions with simple polynomials. Polynomials are very easy to work with and approximations of this sort are often used in Engineering and the Sciences to simplify complex functions. For example sin(x) is often approximated by x when the angle x is small. Let’s remind ourselves of the Taylor series for a single variable. Example 1 Find the Taylor series for g(x) = ex about the point x = 0.

F1 + 1!x + x2! + · · · F

For a function of two variables z = f (x, y), the Taylor Series is a little bit more complicated because we are approximating a surface with simple polynomial functions in x and y. Things to keep in mind are that:

• The Taylor series will involve evaluating partial derivatives of the function f (x, y).

• If we stop the Taylor Series for f (x, y) at some order, we call the resulting polynomial expression the Taylor Series Approximation of the function f (x, y) near the point (a, b).

• The Taylor Series about (a, b) will work best near (a, b)

• The more terms you include the better your approximation will be. Higher-order terms can be calculated using the binomial theorem.

Example 2 Find the Taylor series of f (x, y) = x4 y 3 about the point (1, 2) up to and including quadratic terms.

F 8 + 32(x − 1) + 12(y − 2) + 48(x − 1)2 + 48(x − 1)(y − 2) + 6(y − 2)2 F

Example 3 Calculate the Taylor series of f (x, y) = ex cos(y) about (a, b) = (0, 0) up to and including quadratic terms. Compare the value of f (−0.1, 0.2) with its Taylor approximation.

F 1 + x + 21 (x2 − y 2 ),0.88680091 vs 0.885 F

ERROR ESTIMATES

The first three terms of the Taylor series are a linear approximation of f (x, y),

∂f ∂f f (x, y) ≈ f (a, b) + (x − a) (a, b) + (y − b) (a, b) ∂x ∂y This can be rewritten as ∂f ∂f f (x, y) − f (a, b) ≈ (x − a) (a, b) + (y − b) (a, b) ∂x ∂y and hence ∂f ∂f ∆f ≈ ∆x + ∆y. ∂x ∂y This lovely little equation gives you the change in f given a small change in x and y. This is a very useful result, because it allows us to deal with the case when there is uncertainty in the values of x and y. For example, if there are small errors ∆x and ∆y in x and y, then we can use this formula to estimate the error ∆f in f (x, y)

Example 4 Suppose that temperature T is given by T (x, y) = x2 + y 3 . Find the approximate error in T if x = 1, y = 2, ∆x = 0.1 and ∆y = −0.3.

F − 3.4 F

In the example given, we knew the exact value and sign of the errors ∆x and ∆y. This is generally not the case: usually all we know is the range of possible errors. For example, if we know that our independent variables can take values x ± ∆x and y ± ∆y, then the maximum possible errors in x and y are |∆x| and |∆y|, respectively. The maximum possible error in the function f (x, y) is then given by

∂f ∂f |∆f | ≤ | ||∆x| + | ||∆y|. ∂x ∂y Notice that the absolute value symbols mean we always add together positive numbers!

Example 5 Suppose that temperature T is given by T (x, y) = x2 + y 3 . Find the maximum possible a) absolute error, b) relative absolute error and c) percentage absolute error in T if x = 1, y = 2, |∆x| ≤ 0.1 and |∆y| ≤ 0.3.

F3.8, 3.8 ≈ 0.42, 42%F

Example 6 The volume V of a cone with radius r and perpendicular height h is given by V = πr2 h. Determine the maximum absolute error and the maximum percentage error in calculating V given that r = 5 cm and h = 3 cm to the nearest millimetre.

2.87 F2.87, × 100 ≈ 3.7%F 78.54 You can now do Q 15 to 29
